preamble: The shift from an oral vowel to its nasalized counterpart is a common phonetic process, typically occurring as regressive assimilation when followed by a nasal consonant (VN > 	ilde{V}N), often followed by the loss of the consonant itself.

examples.note: Nasalization of 'i' in French (e.g., 'vin') resulted in [ɛ̃], illustrating the lowering that often accompanies nasalization.
High vowels like 'i' are readily nasalized in Portuguese (e.g., 'fim' [fĩ]).
Hindi demonstrates pervasive vowel nasalization across its inventory as a result of historical nasal consonant absorption.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
